body:
# Multi-User Project & Task Management REST API

## Context

How might we let multiple users manage their own projects and tasks through a
small, dependency-light JSON REST API that survives restarts?

The deliverable is a self-contained Node.js HTTP service (ESM, no external web
framework) that exposes authentication, project, and task endpoints backed by a
persistent on-disk store. It is a greenfield build â€” the repository currently
holds only the Mandrel agent tooling and an empty application surface. Key
assumptions we are betting on: (1) Node's built-in `node:http` server plus manual
routing is sufficient â€” no Express/Fastify; (2) an embedded on-disk store is
acceptable for persistence â€” the built-in `node:sqlite` module (Node 22+) is the
primary choice, with a hand-rolled append/write JSON-file store as the fallback
if `node:sqlite` is unavailable in the runtime; (3) bearer tokens are signed with
an HMAC secret (via `node:crypto`) rather than pulling in a JWT library, though a
tiny signing helper is acceptable; (4) passwords are stored as salted hashes
(`node:crypto` scrypt/pbkdf2), never plaintext. The server binds to `PORT`
(default 3000).

## Goal

A signed-in user can register and log in, then create/list/fetch/delete their own
projects and add/list/update/delete tasks within those projects â€” with
pagination and a done-filter on task listing, and with strict per-user isolation
so no user can see or touch another user's projects or tasks. All data persists
across a server restart, malformed requests never crash the server, and
`npm start` boots the service. The end state is a correct, robust, restartable
API that passes a black-box test of every documented endpoint and its
authorization boundaries.

## Non-Goals

- External web framework (Express, Fastify, Koa) â€” the task explicitly forbids
  one; use `node:http`.
- A front-end / UI â€” this is a JSON API only; there is no HTML surface.
- Third-party auth (OAuth, sessions, refresh tokens) â€” a single HMAC-signed
  bearer token is the whole auth model; keep it minimal.
- Role-based access control or project sharing/collaboration â€” ownership is the
  only authorization axis; an assignee can be another user but does not gain
  access to the project.
- Real-time features (websockets), rate limiting, and email â€” out of scope for
  the MVP and not requested.
- A migration framework or ORM â€” schema is created idempotently on boot; no
  ORM layer.

## Scope

The MVP is one HTTP service with three capability groups plus a persistence and
server-bootstrap foundation:

1. **Persistence & server foundation** â€” on-disk store module (SQLite file or
   equivalent) with idempotent schema init on boot, a `node:http` server with a
   minimal router (method + path-pattern matching, param extraction), JSON body
   parsing with a size cap, centralized error handling so no request crashes the
   process, `PORT` binding (default 3000), and the `npm start` script.
2. **Accounts & auth** â€” `POST /auth/register` (unique username + hashed
   password â†’ `{id, username}`), `POST /auth/login` (â†’ `{token}` HMAC-signed
   bearer), and bearer-token auth middleware that gates every non-auth endpoint
   and resolves the current user.
3. **Projects** â€” `POST /projects`, `GET /projects` (owner-scoped list),
   `GET /projects/:id`, `DELETE /projects/:id` (cascade-deletes its tasks), all
   owner-isolated.
4. **Tasks** â€” `POST /projects/:projectId/tasks` (optional `assigneeId`),
   `GET /projects/:projectId/tasks` (paginated `{items,total,page,pageSize}` with
   `?page`, `?pageSize`, optional `?done=` filter), `PATCH .../:taskId`,
   `DELETE .../:taskId`, all scoped to projects the caller owns.

Sequencing: the foundation lands first (nothing works without the store and
server), then auth (projects/tasks require a resolved user), then projects, then
tasks (tasks live under a project). Isolation and malformed-input robustness are
cross-cutting acceptance requirements verified across every group.

## User Stories

- As a new user, I can register with a username and password and then log in to
  receive a bearer token I send on every subsequent request.
- As a signed-in user, I can create, list, fetch, and delete my own projects,
  and deleting a project removes all of its tasks.
- As a signed-in user, I can add tasks to my project (optionally assigning one to
  another user), list them with pagination and a done-filter, update a task's
  title or done state, and delete a task.
- As any user, I am guaranteed that other users cannot see or modify my projects
  or tasks, and that a malformed request never brings the server down.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `npm start` boots the server on `PORT` (default 3000); data written before
      a restart is still present after it.
- [ ] `POST /auth/register` creates a user and returns `{id, username}`;
      duplicate usernames and missing fields are rejected with a sensible
      4xx (not a crash).
- [ ] `POST /auth/login` returns `{token}` for valid credentials and a 401 for
      bad ones; the token is required (via `Authorization` header) on all
      project/task endpoints, which return 401 when it is missing or invalid.
- [ ] Project CRUD works and is owner-isolated: creating returns
      `{id,name,ownerId,createdAt}`; `GET /projects` lists only the caller's
      projects; fetching or deleting another user's project returns 404/403 and
      never leaks data; deleting a project also deletes its tasks.
- [ ] Task CRUD works within an owned project: create returns
      `{id,title,projectId,assigneeId,createdAt,done:false}`; listing is
      paginated as `{items,total,page,pageSize}` and honors `?page`,
      `?pageSize`, and `?done=true|false`; PATCH updates `title`/`done` and
      returns the updated task; DELETE removes it; all reject access to tasks in
      projects the caller does not own.
- [ ] Malformed requests (invalid JSON, wrong types, missing route, oversized
      body) return a structured JSON error with an appropriate status and never
      crash the server.

> đźŹ·ď¸Ź Authored with Mandrel v0.0.0 Â· 2026-07-12

<!-- meta: {"mandrel_version":"0.0.0","authored_at":"2026-07-12"} -->

<!-- mandrel:tech-spec:start -->

## Delivery Slicing

| Slice | What ships | Independent? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Persistence & Server Foundation | On-disk store module (`src/db.js`) with idempotent schema init on boot, the `node:http` server + minimal router (`src/server.js`, `src/router.js`), JSON body parsing with a size cap, centralized JSON error handling, `PORT` binding (default 3000), and the `npm start` script. | Yes â€” a bootable server with a health path and a live persistent store is a shippable, verifiable increment even before any domain endpoint exists. |
| Accounts & Auth | `POST /auth/register`, `POST /auth/login`, salted password hashing, HMAC-signed bearer tokens, and the auth middleware that gates every non-auth route (`src/auth.js`, `src/routes/auth.js`). | No â€” depends on the Foundation's store and router; without them there is nothing to register against or route through. Justification: dependency (single-consumer of the store/router seam). |
| Projects | Owner-scoped project CRUD â€” `POST /projects`, `GET /projects`, `GET /projects/:id`, `DELETE /projects/:id` (cascade-deletes tasks) (`src/routes/projects.js`). | No â€” depends on Auth to resolve the owning user. Justification: dependency (requires the authenticated-user seam). |
| Tasks | Task CRUD under a project â€” create (optional `assigneeId`), paginated+filtered list, PATCH, DELETE (`src/routes/tasks.js`), all scoped to projects the caller owns. | No â€” depends on Projects (a task lives under a project) and Auth. Justification: dependency (requires the project-ownership seam). |

The count (4) is a ceiling. The three domain slices are dependent single-consumer
links in one chain (Foundation â†’ Auth â†’ Projects â†’ Tasks); they are kept as
distinct slices for **risk isolation** â€” auth/crypto correctness, ownership
isolation, and pagination each carry independent failure modes worth verifying as
their own shippable unit â€” not for parallelism.

## Technical Overview

A single Node.js process using only the standard library: `node:http` for the
transport, a hand-rolled path-pattern router, `node:crypto` for password hashing
(scrypt) and HMAC token signing, and an embedded on-disk store â€” `node:sqlite`
(the built-in SQLite module) when available, degrading to an atomically-written
JSON file store behind the same repository interface when it is not. No runtime
dependencies are added to `package.json`.

## Architecture & Design

- **Layering.** `server.js` (transport + lifecycle) â†’ `router.js` (method/path
  match, param + query extraction) â†’ per-resource route handlers
  (`routes/*.js`) â†’ repository layer (`db.js`) â†’ on-disk store. Handlers never
  touch the store driver directly; they call repository functions so the store
  implementation stays swappable.
- **Store abstraction.** `db.js` exposes a small repository API
  (`createUser`, `findUserByUsername`, `getUserById`, `createProject`,
  `listProjectsByOwner`, `getProject`, `deleteProject`, `createTask`,
  `listTasks`, `getTask`, `updateTask`, `deleteTask`). A capability probe at
  boot selects the `node:sqlite` backend or the JSON-file backend; both
  implement the same interface, so the acceptance criterion "data survives
  restart" holds for either.
- **Routing.** A route table maps `{method, pattern}` â†’ handler. Patterns use
  `:name` segments compiled to a matcher that yields a `params` map. Unknown
  routes return a 404 JSON error; unknown methods on a known path return 405.
- **Request pipeline.** Each request: (1) parse URL + query, (2) match route,
  (3) for protected routes, run auth middleware to resolve `req.user` or return
  401, (4) parse+validate the JSON body (size-capped), (5) invoke the handler
  inside a try/catch that converts any thrown `HttpError` (or unexpected error)
  into a structured JSON response so a single bad request can never crash the
  process.
- **Config.** `PORT` from `process.env.PORT` (default 3000); the token signing
  secret from `process.env.AUTH_SECRET` with a documented dev default; the
  store file path from `process.env.DATA_FILE`/`DATA_DIR` (default under the
  repo, e.g. `./data/app.db` or `./data/store.json`).

## Data Models

- **User** â€” `id` (PK), `username` (unique, indexed), `passwordHash`
  (`scrypt` digest), `salt`, `createdAt`.
- **Project** â€” `id` (PK), `name`, `ownerId` (FK â†’ User.id, indexed),
  `createdAt`.
- **Task** â€” `id` (PK), `title`, `projectId` (FK â†’ Project.id, indexed),
  `assigneeId` (nullable FK â†’ User.id), `done` (boolean, default false),
  `createdAt`.

Cascade: deleting a Project deletes its Tasks (ON DELETE CASCADE under SQLite;
an explicit delete-children step in the JSON-file backend). IDs are opaque
strings (e.g. `crypto.randomUUID()`) or monotonic integers â€” consistent within
a backend.

## API Changes

New surface (all JSON; protected routes require `Authorization: Bearer <token>`):

- `POST /auth/register` â†’ `201 {id, username}`; 400 on missing fields, 409 on
  duplicate username.
- `POST /auth/login` â†’ `200 {token}`; 401 on bad credentials.
- `POST /projects` â†’ `201 {id,name,ownerId,createdAt}`.
- `GET /projects` â†’ `200 [ ...owner's projects ]`.
- `GET /projects/:id` â†’ `200 {project}` or 404 (also 404 for another user's
  project â€” no existence leak).
- `DELETE /projects/:id` â†’ `204`; cascade-deletes tasks.
- `POST /projects/:projectId/tasks` â†’ `201 {id,title,projectId,assigneeId,createdAt,done:false}`.
- `GET /projects/:projectId/tasks?page=&pageSize=&done=` â†’
  `200 {items,total,page,pageSize}`.
- `PATCH /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId` â†’ `200 {updated task}`.
- `DELETE /projects/:projectId/tasks/:taskId` â†’ `204`.

## Core Components

- `src/server.js` â€” `node:http` server creation, `PORT` binding, request
  dispatch, top-level error boundary, graceful shutdown.
- `src/router.js` â€” route registration + `:param` pattern matching + query
  parsing.
- `src/db.js` â€” backend probe (`node:sqlite` vs JSON file), idempotent schema
  init, repository API.
- `src/auth.js` â€” `scrypt` password hash/verify, HMAC token sign/verify, auth
  middleware resolving the current user.
- `src/routes/auth.js`, `src/routes/projects.js`, `src/routes/tasks.js` â€”
  resource handlers with ownership enforcement.
- `src/http-error.js` â€” `HttpError` class + `sendJson` helper for uniform
  responses.
- `src/index.js` â€” entrypoint wired to the `npm start` script.

All paths above are **net-new** (greenfield repository; `codebaseSnapshot.files`
is empty).

## Security & Privacy Considerations

- **Password storage.** Never store plaintext; hash with `scrypt` + a
  per-user random salt via `node:crypto`; compare with `timingSafeEqual`.
- **Tokens.** Bearer token is an HMAC-SHA256 signature (keyed by
  `AUTH_SECRET`) over a compact payload (userId + issued-at); verify signature
  and reject tampered/expired tokens with 401. No sensitive data in the token
  body beyond the user id.
- **Ownership isolation.** Every project/task read and write is filtered by the
  authenticated `ownerId`; cross-user access returns 404 (no existence leak)
  rather than 403 where disclosure would matter. `assigneeId` grants no access
  to the project â€” assignment is a data field only.
- **Input hardening.** Cap request body size, reject non-JSON / malformed JSON
  with 400, coerce/validate types (e.g. `page`/`pageSize` numeric bounds), and
  never interpolate untrusted input into SQL â€” use parameterized statements in
  the SQLite backend.

<!-- mandrel:tech-spec:end -->
title: Multi-User Project & Task Management REST API
